agent_output:
```yaml
handoff_brief:
  recipient: Shaq
  task_context: >
    Implement the notification preferences feature for the user API. Users can
    manage their marketing email preferences. Transactional emails (password
    resets, security alerts) cannot be disabled under any circumstances — this
    is a legal requirement surfaced during Bird's domain review. The feature
    exposes a PATCH endpoint and must enforce the transactional email constraint
    at the API layer with a specific error response.

  domain_rules:
    - rule: Marketing email opt-out is permitted
      testable_assertion: >
        A PATCH request setting marketing_emails_enabled=false must succeed
        and persist the preference
    - rule: Transactional emails cannot be disabled under any circumstances
      testable_assertion: >
        A PATCH request setting transactional_emails_enabled=false must be
        rejected — no partial update, no silent ignore

  architecture_guidance:
    - decision: PATCH /users/{id}/notification-preferences
      implementation_note: >
        Partial update semantics — only fields present in the request body
        are modified. Missing fields are left unchanged.
    - decision: marketing_emails_enabled stored as boolean
      implementation_note: >
        Persisted directly on the user preferences record. No additional
        enum or category mapping required.
    - decision: transactional_emails_enabled validated at API layer
      implementation_note: >
        If the request body includes transactional_emails_enabled=false,
        reject immediately with HTTP 403 before any write occurs.

  acceptance_criteria:
    - criterion: Marketing opt-out succeeds
      given: A user with marketing_emails_enabled=true
      when: PATCH /users/{id}/notification-preferences with { marketing_emails_enabled: false }
      then: 200 returned, marketing_emails_enabled persisted as false

    - criterion: Transactional opt-out is blocked
      given: A user who attempts to disable transactional emails via the API
      when: PATCH /users/{id}/notification-preferences with { transactional_emails_enabled: false }
      then: >
        HTTP 403 returned with error code TRANSACTIONAL_OPT_OUT_PROHIBITED;
        no write occurs; existing preferences unchanged

  terminology_alignment:
    - domain_term: "opt out of marketing emails"
      technical_term: marketing_emails_enabled=false
      definition: Boolean field on the user preferences record; false means opted out
    - domain_term: "transactional emails cannot be disabled"
      technical_term: transactional_emails_enabled cannot be set to false
      definition: >
        The field may exist in the data model but the API must reject any
        request that attempts to set it to false

  contradictions_resolved:
    - conflict: >
        Bird v1 included AC2 (user can opt out of transactional emails).
        Bird v2 removed AC2 and added AC3 (403 on attempt). Both versions
        were submitted.
      resolution: >
        Bird v2 supersedes v1. The removal of AC2 was driven by legal review —
        transactional email opt-out is not permitted under any circumstances.
        AC3 is the authoritative requirement. v1 AC2 is discarded entirely.

  open_questions:
    - >
      Scope of transactional_emails_enabled in the data model: should the
      field exist at all (hardcoded to true), or be absent from the schema?
      MJ did not specify — either works for the 403 behavior but affects
      future extensibility.
    - >
      Should a PATCH that includes transactional_emails_enabled=true (i.e.,
      asserting the current state) be accepted as a no-op or also rejected?
      Bird and MJ are silent on this edge case.
    - >
      Authorization: does the endpoint require the caller to be the user
      themselves, or can admins also modify preferences? Not specified by
      Bird or MJ.
```

**Note on Bird's versioning**: v2 was submitted after v1 and is the authoritative domain specification. v1's AC2 (transactional opt-out permitted) is fully superseded. The change was not a refinement — it reversed a permission based on a legal constraint. Shaq should treat v1 as void.
